oai_dc:dc.dc:title: The Plight of the Grey-cheeked Parakeet
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Robinson, Sheryl
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This item originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 51 Number 1 (January/February 2024).The following is an excerpt from the full PDF link.by Sheryl Robinson Grey-cheeked Parakeets are part of the genus Brotogeris, which includes 7 other species. They are the White -winged parakeet (Brotogeris versicolurus), Orange-chinned parakeet (Brotogeris jugulari), Golden-winged parakeet (Brotogeris chrysoptera), Plain parakeet (Brotogeris tirica), Cobalt -winged parakeet, (Brotogeris cyanoptera), Yellow -chevroned parakeet (aka Canary -winged)1 (Brotogeris chiriri), and the Tui parakeet (Brotogeris sanctithomae). The most common species found in U.S. aviculture were the Canary -wings, Grey -cheeks and Orange -chinned. Many Grey - cheeks were imported during the 1980’s, but these cute little birds proved challenging to breed. Currently all Brotogeris birds are rare to find, especially as babies.The Grey -cheeked Parakeet (Brotogeris pyrrhoptera) is native to West Ecuador and Northwest Peru. They are considered vulnerable in their native habitat. Typically, they nest in termite mounds and are community nesters.They range from 7.5 to 8.1 inches long and weigh between 60 and 68 grams. Their coloration is distinctly different from other Brotogeris species. They have light turquoise on their head, grey colored cheeks, orange feathers under their wings and iridescent green feathers on their back, outer wings and tail areas, with a lime -green shade on their belly. They hatch with a dark beak which becomes flesh colored around 6 months of age. They have also been called pocket parrots, orange - flanked parakeets, orange -winged parakeetsClick the PDF link associated with this item to view the complete item as originally published.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 51 No. 1 (2024): Watchbird January/February 2024; 22-23
